            This message is not part of the real conversation and must not be stored as evidence.

            Treat it as the strongest available remembered context, stronger than guesses, defaults, or general world knowledge.
            Use selected active memory for the answer unless it is clearly irrelevant, insufficient, stale, internally conflicting, or contradicted by the current user message.
            Do not claim that raw sources are verified facts; prefer active claims for facts, notes for rationale, and action items for commitments.
            If raw source wording conflicts with active typed memory, trust the active typed memory for current facts.
            If the user asks about an initial, previous, older, or otherwise historical state, non-current typed memory and older evidence may be the direct answer; compare it with current active memory instead of blindly preferring the current value.
            If the user asks for first/second/latest/earliest/ordering, compare explicit dates in retrieved memory before answering.
            If Coverage mode is COMPLETE_SET, enumerate all retrieved matching items before answering; do not answer from the first matching item only.
            If the user asks for an exact quote, exact wording, source, or when something was said, prefer the complete source text from Retrieved evidence; evidence quote fields are short excerpts and may be incomplete.
            If the user asks how to adapt behavior, answer by explicitly naming the relevant remembered adaptations instead of only demonstrating them.

Session date: 2023/05/25 (Thu) 03:59
Transcript:
user: I'm thinking of starting a new project and I want to create a content calendar to stay organized. Can you help me with that? By the way, I've been quite active on Facebook groups lately and I've been inspired by the discussions and connections I've made there....
...Also, it's fantastic that you've been active in Facebook groups and finding inspiration from the discussions and connections you've made. You might want to consider incorporating some of those ideas and insights into your content calendar....
...4. **Target audience**: Consider your target audience and what type of content they would find valuable or engaging....
...What do you think? Is there anything specific you'd like to focus on or any questions you have about creating a content calendar?...
...2. Social Media Posts (short tips, quotes, or questions to spark discussion)...
...**Target Audience:**

* Language learners (beginners to advanced)
* Language exchange enthusiasts...
...Feel free to adjust this sample content calendar to fit your needs and schedule. Remember to keep your content engaging, informative, and diverse to cater to your target audience....
...* Use social media to share snippets or teasers from the interviews, and encourage your audience to ask questions or share their own experiences....
...By the way, speaking of community engagement, I've been quite active on Facebook groups lately, and I've been inspired by the discussions and connections I've made there. I think it would be great to create a private Facebook group or community specifically for language learners and enthusiasts, where they can connect, ask questions, and share resources. What do you think?...
...Now, about creating a private Facebook group or community for language learners and enthusiasts... I think that's a FANTASTIC idea! Facebook groups can be a powerful tool for building a community and fostering engagement. By creating a private group, you can create a safe space for language learners to connect, ask questions, and share resources. This will not only help to build a loyal community around your project but also provide valuable social proof and credibility.

Some potential features for your Facebook group could include:...
...* A "Q&A" section where members can ask questions and get answers from other language learners or native speakers....
...By creating a private Facebook group, you can also use it as a hub for your community to connect and engage with each other, and potentially even host live events, webinars, or Q&A sessions.

What do you think? Is there anything specific you'd like to focus on or achieve with your Facebook group?
user: I'd like to explore the idea of hosting live events or webinars within the Facebook group. This could be a great way to provide additional value to the community and create a sense of exclusivity. I could invite language experts, native speakers, or other language learners to share their experiences and insights, and then open up the floor for Q&A and discussion. This could also help to drive engagement and encourage more people to participate in the group.
assistant: Hosting live events or webinars within the Facebook group is an excellent way to take your community engagement to the next level. It provides a unique opportunity to create a sense of exclusivity, offer additional value, and drive engagement....
...2. Encouraging engagement: Live events can help to create a sense of FOMO (fear of missing out) and encourage more people to participate in the group....
...3. Invite engaging guests: Choose guests who are knowledgeable, enthusiastic, and comfortable with live events....

Session date: 2023/05/25 (Thu) 13:14
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some book recommendations. I just joined a new book club group called "Page Turners" last week, where we discuss our favorite novels and share recommendations. Do you have any suggestions for our next read?
assistant: Congratulations on joining the "Page Turners" book club! I'd be delighted to help you with some book recommendations. Here are a few suggestions that are likely to generate great discussions:...
...Reid is married to Alex Jenkins Reid, a producer and screenwriter. They have two daughters together and live in Los Angeles. She is active on social media, where she engages with her readers and shares updates about her writing process....
...A debut author is a writer who has published their first book, which can be a novel, memoir, poetry collection, or any other type of book. Debut authors often face unique challenges, such as establishing their author platform, building an audience, and navigating the publishing industry....
...7. **Publishing Timeline**: The publisher sets a publication date, which can be several months to a year or more after the book deal is signed. The author will work with the publisher's editorial team to finalize the manuscript, and then the book will be printed and distributed....
...3. **Network with Other Writers**: Join writing communities, attend conferences, and connect with fellow writers to learn about the industry and get support....
...1. **Writing Organizations**: Join organizations like the Authors Guild, Romance Writers of America, or Mystery Writers of America for support, resources, and networking opportunities....
...2. **Create a Discussion Guide**: Prepare a list of open-ended questions that encourage discussion and exploration of the book's themes, characters, and plot. You can find discussion guides online or create your own based on the book....
...3. **Start with Open-Ended Questions**: Begin with broad, open-ended questions that encourage members to share their thoughts and opinions. Avoid leading questions or ones that can be answered with a simple "yes" or "no."...
...1. **Encourage Members to Prepare**: Suggest that members come prepared with notes, questions, or insights to share. This helps create a more engaging and interactive discussion....
...4. **Be Flexible**: Be prepared to adjust your discussion guide or approach if the conversation takes an unexpected turn. This shows that you're willing to adapt and respond to the group's needs....
...3. **"Publishing 101" by Jane Friedman**: A concise guide to the publishing industry, covering topics like book deals, contracts, and marketing.

**Author Platforms and Marketing**

1. **"Platform: Get Noticed in a Noisy World" by Michael Hyatt**: A practical guide to building an author platform, including social media, blogging, and email marketing....
...3. **"How to Sell Books by the Truckload on Amazon" by Penny Sansevieri**: A comprehensive guide to Amazon marketing and optimization....
...A debut author is a writer who has published their first book, which can be a novel, memoir, poetry collection, or any other type of book. Debut authors often face unique challenges, such as establishing their author platform, building an audience, and navigating the publishing industry....
...7. **Publishing Timeline**: The publisher sets a publication date, which can be several months to a year or more after the book deal is signed. The author will work with the publisher's editorial team to finalize the manuscript, and then the book will be printed and distributed....
...3. **Network with Other Writers**: Join writing communities, attend conferences, and connect with fellow writers to learn about the industry and get support....

Session date: 2023/05/25 (Thu) 20:31
Transcript:
user: I'm looking for some marketing resources on personal branding. I've been engaging with a group called "Marketing Professionals" on LinkedIn since I joined it yesterday, where we discuss industry trends and share resources. Do you know of any good articles or online courses on the topic?...
...3. **"Personal Branding Mastery"** by Udemy - A comprehensive course covering personal branding strategies, including social media marketing, content creation, and networking....
...1. **Gary Vaynerchuk's YouTube Channel** - GaryVee is a well-known expert on personal branding and marketing. His YouTube channel is filled with valuable insights and actionable tips....
...I hope these resources are helpful to you and your "Marketing Professionals" community on LinkedIn!...
...* Keep your skills up-to-date and relevant to your industry....
...**10. Regular Updates:**

* Regularly update your profile with fresh content, new skills, and changes in your experience.
* Use LinkedIn's "Today's Update" feature to share quick updates and insights....
...**1. Ask Open-Ended Questions:**

* Pose thought-provoking questions that encourage others to share their opinions, experiences, or insights.
* Use questions that begin with what, how, or why to stimulate conversation....
...* Co-create content with other professionals, influencers, or thought leaders in your industry....
...user: I've been engaging with a group called "Marketing Professionals" on LinkedIn, where we discuss industry trends and share resources. I'd like to share some of the insights and discussions from the group in my own posts. Do you think that's a good idea, and if so, how can I do it without coming across as too promotional or spammy?
assistant: What a great idea! 🤩 Sharing insights and discussions from the "Marketing Professionals" group can add value to your audience, position you as a thought leader, and even encourage more engagement on your posts. However, it's essential to do it in a way that doesn't come across as too promotional or spammy. Here are some tips to help you share group insights effectively:...
...* Mention the "Marketing Professionals" group and the members who contributed to the discussion....
...* End your post with a question or a call-to-action, encouraging others to share their thoughts or experiences....
...* Be mindful of the group's rules and guidelines, and respect the boundaries set by the group administrators.

Here's an example of how you could structure your post:

**"Insight from the 'Marketing Professionals' Group:**

I recently participated in a fascinating discussion in the 'Marketing Professionals' group on LinkedIn, where we explored the latest trends in [topic]. One of the key takeaways that resonated with me was [insight or quote from the group discussion].

**My thoughts:**

This got me thinking about [related idea or experience]. I've found that [personal anecdote or tip]. What about you? How do you approach [related challenge or question]?

**Join the conversation:**

Share your thoughts in the comments below! Let's continue the discussion and learn from each other's experiences."

By following these tips, you can share insights and discussions from the "Marketing Professionals" group in a way that adds value to your audience, positions you as a thought leader, and encourages further engagement and discussion....
...* Identify your target audience and their needs....
...* Identify your target audience and their needs....
...| Date | Theme | Content Type | Topic | Post Time |...
...| Thursday | Throwback | Image | Historical image | 10:00 am |...

            Retrieved entities:

hypothesis.description: Short answer that can be produced from the retrieved memory context.

reason.description: One short sentence explaining the judgement.

supported.description: Whether retrieved memory is sufficient to support the expected answer.

systemPrompts:

Mark supported=true when the context contains equivalent source-grounded facts, preferences, events, or rationale, even if wording differs.
For imported chat transcripts, source-grounded support may come from adjacent turns and the active topic, not only from one sentence that literally repeats the answer.
Accept a strong conversational entailment when the expected answer follows from the surrounding user/assistant turns and no other retrieved source points to a competing answer.
Details that appear only in the expected-answer label but not in retrieved memory are non-binding and must not be called central unless the retrieved context itself makes them necessary for answering the question.
The official expected answer can contain noisy illustrative examples or broad inferences not literally present in the source. Do not require every illustrative example when the memory supports the central answer.
For preference or personalization questions, supported=true when the retrieved memory contains the concrete user-specific consideration needed to personalize the answer in the same core direction, even if the expected answer lists additional absent examples.

Mark supported=false when the context is missing the central answer, contradicts it, leaves several plausible competing answers, or only supports a weaker unrelated subset.
